content:
1	# Second Brain Wiki Configuration
2	
3	This file configures the skill for Evan's environment. Edit paths and
4	projects as your work evolves.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Paths
9	
10	| Path | Value | Description |
11	|------|-------|-------------|
12	| Wiki directory | `~/second-brain/wiki/` | Where wiki pages live |
13	| Sources directory | `~/second-brain/sources/` | Raw source files stored here |
14	| Inbox directory | `~/Desktop/Second Brain Inbox/` | Drop files here for processing |
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Active Projects
19	
20	These are your current projects and areas of focus. The skill uses these
21	to find connections when ingesting new content.
22	
23	- **evaluator-super-agent** — automated prototype quality assessment using persona-driven walkthroughs, 7-dimension rubric scoring, actor/evaluator separation
24	- **prototype-creator** — generates clickable prototypes from RFEs, tiered AC classification, Playwright automation
25	- **agent-eval-harness** — framework for evaluating AI skill quality, eval.yaml configs, judges, scoring pipelines
26	- **ai-sdlc-pipeline** — three-phase pipeline: RFE Creator (WHAT) → Prototype Creator (SHOW) → Strat Creator (HOW)
27	- **my-cursor-claw** — personal workspace with 20+ skills, MCP connections, notetaking, automations
28	- **decision-kit** — Decision Driven Development toolkit, /strategize, /decide, /journal
29	- **automated-usability-testing** — simulated usability testing tool, persona YAML files, evaluate-flow protocol
30	- **archie** — UX research agent for the RHOAIUX team
31	- **rhoai-prototypes** — RHOAI prototype collection for evaluation
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Calendar Integration
36	
37	| Setting | Value |
38	|---------|-------|
39	| Calendar name | `primary` |
40	| Auto-ingest meetings | `true` |
41	| Minimum meeting duration | `15 min` |
42	| Require notes/transcript | `true` |
43	| Skip recurring without notes | `true` |
44	
45	Events that qualify for auto-ingestion:
46	- Have attached meeting notes or a description with substance (not just "Weekly sync")
47	- Have a linked Google Doc (agenda, transcript, action items)
48	- Duration is 15+ minutes
49	- Are not recurring meetings that had no notes added
50	
51	---
52	
53	## MCP Integrations
54	
55	| Integration | MCP Server | Used for |
56	|-------------|-----------|----------|
57	| Google Docs | `user-google-workspace` | Extracting doc content |
58	| Google Calendar | `user-google-workspace` | Meeting ingestion |
59	| Slack | `project-0-my-cursor-claw-slack` | Channel message ingestion |
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Cross-Link Priority
64	
65	When multiple connections exist, prioritize these categories (from the
66	wiki index):
67	
68	1. **Red Hat AI-SDLC** — pipeline components, RFE/prototype/strat flow
69	2. **Evaluation & Quality** — rubrics, heuristics, eval harness
70	3. **Tools & Skills** — decision kit, cursor claw, skill overlays
71	4. **Meetings** — meeting notes with action items and decisions
72	5. **Standups** — sprint progress and blockers
73	6. **Session Logs** — what was built and shipped
74

1	# Wiki Page Template
2	
3	Use this template when creating new wiki pages. Copy the structure,
4	fill in the bracketed fields.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Template
9	
10	```markdown
11	# <Page Title>
12	
13	<One-sentence summary of what this page covers.>
14	
15	## Key Points
16	
17	- <Bullet point 1: a concrete fact, concept, or takeaway>
18	- <Bullet point 2>
19	- <Bullet point 3>
20	- <Additional bullets as needed, max 10>
21	
22	## Related
23	
24	- [<Related Page Title>](<related-slug>.md) — <one-line reason for connection>
25	- [<Another Related Page>](<slug>.md) — <connection reason>
26	
27	## Sources
28	
29	- <path/to/source/file or URL where this came from>
30	- Ingested: <YYYY-MM-DD>
31	```
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Good Example
36	
37	```markdown
38	# Retrieval Augmented Generation
39	
40	Technique that grounds LLM responses in retrieved documents rather than
41	relying solely on training data.
42	
43	## Key Points
44	
45	- Combines a retriever (vector search) with a generator (LLM)
46	- Reduces hallucination by providing source documents as context
47	- Trade-off: retrieval quality limits generation quality
48	- Common stack: embeddings → vector DB → reranker → LLM prompt
49	- Works best when source corpus is well-chunked and up-to-date
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [Vector Databases](vector-databases.md) — the retrieval layer in RAG pipelines
54	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — uses embedding similarity for judge calibration
55	
56	## Sources
57	
58	- ~/second-brain/sources/what-is-rag.md
59	- Ingested: 2026-06-10
60	```
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Bad Example
65	
66	```markdown
67	# RAG
68	
69	Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique in artificial intelligence
70	that combines information retrieval with text generation. It was first proposed
71	by Lewis et al. in 2020. The basic idea is that instead of relying solely on
72	what a language model learned during training, you can augment it with relevant
73	documents retrieved at inference time.
74	
75	This approach has several advantages. First, it reduces hallucination because
76	the model can reference actual documents. Second, it allows the model to access
77	up-to-date information that wasn't in its training data. Third, it provides
78	transparency because you can trace responses back to source documents.
79	
80	There are several implementations of RAG available today...
81	```
82	
83	**Why it's bad:**
84	- Reads like a Wikipedia article, not a wiki node
85	- No bullet summaries (walls of prose)
86	- No Related section (isolated page, no connections)
87	- No Sources section (where did this come from?)
88	- Title is an acronym without expansion
89	- Contains filler ("There are several..." "This approach has...")
90

text: There's already a `vector-databases.md` and `vector-similarity-search.md`. Let me read those to check for overlap.

content:
1	# Vector Databases
2	
3	A vector database stores data as high-dimensional vectors (embeddings) and enables fast similarity search. They are a key component in [RAG](retrieval-augmented-generation.md) pipelines, recommendation systems, and semantic search.
4	
5	## Popular Options
6	
7	- **ChromaDB** — open-source, easy to set up, Python-native
8	- **Pinecone** — managed service, scales automatically
9	- **Weaviate** — open-source, supports hybrid search (vector + keyword)
10	- **Qdrant** — open-source, Rust-based, high performance
11	- **pgvector** — PostgreSQL extension; adds vector storage and ANN search to an existing Postgres DB
12	
13	## How They Work
14	
15	1. Documents are converted to embeddings using a model
16	2. Embeddings are indexed for fast nearest-neighbor search
17	3. At query time, the query is embedded and compared to stored vectors
18	4. The most similar vectors (and their associated documents) are returned
19	
20	## Use Cases
21	
22	- [RAG systems](retrieval-augmented-generation.md) for grounding LLM responses
23	- Semantic search across large document collections
24	- Recommendation engines
25	- Image and audio similarity search
26	
27	## Related
28	
29	- [Vector Similarity Search](vector-similarity-search.md) — the algorithms (HNSW, IVF) that power the nearest-neighbor queries vector DBs run
30	- [Retrieval-Augmented Generation](retrieval-augmented-generation.md) — primary consumer of vector DB retrieval
31	
32	## Sources
33	
34	- sources/vector-databases.md
35	- Ingested: 2026-07-09 (updated: added pgvector)
36

1	# Vector Similarity Search
2	
3	Core technique for finding semantically similar content using vector embeddings — the engine behind modern search, recommendations, and deduplication.
4	
5	## Key Points
6	
7	- Text is transformed into dense vector embeddings via transformer models (e.g. OpenAI, Cohere, sentence-transformers)
8	- Similarity measured with cosine similarity (angle between vectors) or dot product (magnitude-aware)
9	- Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms trade small accuracy losses for large speed gains
10	  - **HNSW** (Hierarchical Navigable Small Worlds) — graph-based, best recall/speed trade-off
11	  - **IVF** (Inverted File Index) — cluster-based, efficient at massive scale
12	- Exact search is O(n) per query; ANN brings this closer to O(log n)
13	- Powers: RAG retrieval, recommendation engines, semantic search, duplicate detection
14	
15	## Related
16	
17	- [Vector Databases](vector-databases.md) — specialized stores that implement ANN indexing at scale
18	- [Retrieval-Augmented Generation](retrieval-augmented-generation.md) — uses vector similarity as its retrieval step
19	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — similarity search is applicable for deduplicating test cases and detecting redundant eval inputs
20	
21	## Sources
22	
23	- Ingested: 2026-07-09
24

text: The existing `vector-databases.md` covers all submitted key points, and `vector-similarity-search.md` handles HNSW and cosine similarity in depth. The content has HIGH overlap. I'll merge the one piece of value — explicit term definitions — into the existing page rather than creating a duplicate.

input.new_string:
## Key Concepts

- **Embeddings** — dense numerical representations of text, images, or other content; the format vector DBs store and search
- **Cosine similarity** — primary distance metric; measures the angle between vectors regardless of magnitude
- **HNSW** — the ANN algorithm most vector DBs use; see [Vector Similarity Search](vector-similarity-search.md) for detail
- **Speed vs. recall trade-off** — exact search is precise but O(n); approximate (ANN) is fast but may miss the nearest neighbor

## Popular Options
